title: Acute exacerbation of chronic RLQ pain with benzodiazepine misuse and combative behavior in a middle-aged woman with rheumatic heart disease
doorChart: 58-year-old female — RLQ pain with medication misuse and agitation in ED — ESI 2
chiefComplaint: Worsening right lower quadrant pain and acute sedative misuse with escalating behavior in the ED
pmh: Chronic pelvic and abdominal pain; rheumatic heart disease with mitral valve replacement; morbid obesity; type 2 diabetes; peripheral vascular disease; COPD; history of benzodiazepine use disorder with prior rehab admissions

patientProfile.arrivalMode: arrived by EMS after family called 911 due to altered behavior at home; became increasingly agitated in triage
patientProfile.historyOfPresentIllness: I've had this deep aching pain in my right lower belly for about three years now, ever since my hysterectomy. It flares up and nothing really touches it. Today it got so bad I took extra clonazepam — maybe ten or twelve of them — because I just couldn't cope anymore and I wanted someone to finally listen. My daughter called the ambulance. When I got here I started yelling because they made me wait and I could tell nobody was taking me seriously. I know I shouldn't have taken all those pills but I wasn't trying to hurt myself, I just wanted the pain to stop. I've had my mitral valve replaced and I'm on blood thinners, so I know things can get complicated. I don't have any chest pain or fever right now. I just want someone to fix this pain once and for all. After I calmed down a bit they put me on a monitor but then I got upset again when they said I had to wait for psych. I pulled out my IV and tried to leave, so they had to restrain me. I feel bad about that now. I know I need help but I'm frustrated.
patientProfile.socialHistory: Never smoked; occasional wine drinker in the past, now abstinent; divorced; lives with adult daughter; former administrative assistant; limited mobility due to chronic pain and obesity
patientProfile.medications: Clonazepam 1 mg TID; Gabapentin 600 mg TID; Warfarin 5 mg daily; Metformin 1000 mg BID; Lisinopril 20 mg daily; Albuterol inhaler PRN; Omeprazole 40 mg daily
patientProfile.personalityNotes: Alternates between tearful cooperation and hostile outbursts; responds well to female staff; requires firm but empathetic communication; calms significantly when she feels heard

physicalExam.general: Overweight female appearing older than stated age; tearful at times, intermittently combative; resting comfortably when undisturbed
physicalExam.heent: Oropharynx clear; mucous membranes moist; no facial trauma
physicalExam.cardiovascular: Mechanical click heard consistent with prosthetic mitral valve; regular rate; no gallops or rubs
physicalExam.respiratory: Scattered expiratory wheezes bilaterally; no crackles; mildly increased work of breathing during agitation episodes
physicalExam.abdomen: RLQ tenderness to deep palpation over surgical scar; abdomen obese, soft, no rebound or guarding; no palpable masses; normoactive bowel sounds
physicalExam.neurological: Alert and oriented x3 when calm; mildly sedated after benzodiazepine ingestion but arousable; no focal deficits; pupils equal and reactive
physicalExam.musculoskeletal: Bilateral lower extremity edema 1+; no acute joint swelling or deformity
physicalExam.skin: Warm; well-healed midline abdominal scar; no rashes or breakdown

labs.name: Metabolic panel, comprehensive
labs.result: Glucose 189 (elevated); BUN 22; Creatinine 1.1; all other components within normal limits

imaging.name: CT abdomen/pelvis with contrast
imaging.result: Post-hysterectomy surgical changes in the pelvis with mild pelvic adhesive disease. No acute appendicitis, bowel obstruction, or free fluid. Small right inguinal hernia containing fat only. Hepatic steatosis. Mild bilateral renal cysts. No lymphadenopathy or mass lesion identified. Scattered colonic diverticula without inflammation.

goldStandard.presentation: A 58-year-old woman with chronic right lower quadrant pain attributed to post-hysterectomy adhesive disease presents after intentionally ingesting a large quantity of her prescribed clonazepam out of frustration with inadequate pain control. She has a complex medical history including a mechanical mitral valve on anticoagulation, COPD, and type 2 diabetes. In the ED she exhibited escalating agitation requiring physical restraints after removing her IV and attempting to leave. She denies suicidal intent, framing the overdose as a cry for help regarding her pain. Vitals are stable with mild tachycardia and hypertension. CT imaging shows no acute abdominal pathology. INR is therapeutic. Psychiatry consultation was initiated for risk assessment.
goldStandard.diagnosis: Chronic right lower quadrant pain likely secondary to post-surgical adhesive disease; benzodiazepine overdose with behavioral crisis; comorbid rheumatic heart disease status post mechanical mitral valve replacement on therapeutic anticoagulation; no acute surgical abdomen on imaging
goldStandard.disposition: Held in ED for observation and psychiatric clearance; discharged with outpatient pain management referral and psychiatric follow-up; warfarin management unchanged; instructed to return for worsening pain, bleeding, altered mental status, or suicidal ideation; safety plan documented with patient and daughter
goldStandard.mdm: The patient presented with a chronic pain exacerbation complicated by intentional benzodiazepine overdose and behavioral escalation. CT abdomen/pelvis showed no acute surgical pathology, consistent with her known post-hysterectomy adhesive disease. INR remained in therapeutic range for her mechanical valve, and no bleeding complications were identified. Toxicology confirmed benzodiazepine exposure without co-ingestants. After de-escalation, the patient was evaluated by psychiatry and determined to be low acute risk for self-harm, with the overdose characterized as impulsive and pain-driven rather than a true suicide attempt. The care team coordinated with her outpatient pain specialist for regimen adjustment and arranged psychiatric follow-up. She was discharged with a documented safety plan, return precautions, and family involvement in aftercare.

title: Volume Depletion with Near-Syncope in a 74-Year-Old Male
doorChart: Male, 70s — persistent vomiting, loose stools, and lightheadedness — ESI 3
chiefComplaint: vomiting and diarrhea with near-syncopal episodes
pmh: Adrenal insufficiency on chronic glucocorticoid replacement; atrial flutter status post ablation; type 2 diabetes; benign prostatic hypertrophy; osteoarthritis; remote history of colon polyps

patientProfile.historyOfPresentIllness: I woke up Thursday morning feeling queasy and by the afternoon I was vomiting every couple of hours. By Friday morning the diarrhea started — loose and watery, no blood. I nearly passed out twice getting up from a chair, both times I caught myself on the armrest and sat back down. My wife says I looked pale and was barely drinking anything. I ate leftover shrimp stir-fry Wednesday night and I'm wondering if that's what set this off. I've been trying to keep up with my medications but I threw up my morning pills both days. I take hydrocortisone for my adrenals and I know missing doses can be dangerous. No chest pain, no headache, no belly pain really — just waves of nausea. I feel weak and washed out.
patientProfile.socialHistory: Never smoked; social drinker, maybe one beer a week; married 48 years; lives with wife in single-story home; retired electrician; walks 20 minutes daily when feeling well
patientProfile.medications: Hydrocortisone 20 mg morning / 10 mg evening; Metformin 500 mg BID; Tamsulosin 0.4 mg daily; Flecainide 100 mg BID; Aspirin 81 mg daily; Vitamin D 2000 IU daily
patientProfile.personalityNotes: Quiet and stoic; minimizes symptoms; wife provides most of the history; cooperative and appreciative of care

physicalExam.general: Alert, oriented, reclining in bed, appears fatigued but not acutely distressed
physicalExam.heent: Oropharynx dry; mucous membranes tacky; sunken eyes; no oral lesions
physicalExam.cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm; no murmurs; capillary refill 3 seconds peripherally
physicalExam.respiratory: Clear bilaterally; no adventitious sounds; comfortable on room air
physicalExam.abdomen: Soft, nontender, nondistended; hyperactive bowel sounds; no guarding or rebound
physicalExam.neurological: GCS 15; cranial nerves intact; no focal weakness; mildly slow to respond but appropriate
physicalExam.musculoskeletal: No peripheral edema; mild bilateral knee crepitus; no focal tenderness
physicalExam.skin: Tenting over dorsum of hands; warm; no rash or bruising

labs.name: CMP, comprehensive
labs.result: Sodium 128; Potassium 3.2; Chloride 91; CO2 21; BUN 38; Creatinine 1.7 (baseline 1.0); Glucose 78; Calcium 8.4; Albumin 3.7

labs.name: Cortisol, random
labs.flag: low — consistent with adrenal insufficiency and missed replacement doses

imaging.name: ECG 12-lead
imaging.result: Sinus rhythm at 80 bpm; no ST changes; no Q waves; normal intervals

goldStandard.presentation: A 74-year-old man with known adrenal insufficiency on chronic glucocorticoid replacement presents with two days of vomiting and watery diarrhea with two near-syncopal episodes upon standing. He reports inability to keep oral medications down, including his hydrocortisone. Exam reveals signs of moderate dehydration with dry mucous membranes, delayed capillary refill, and skin tenting. Labs demonstrate hyponatremia, hypokalemia, hypomagnesemia, prerenal azotemia, and a critically low random cortisol level consistent with impending adrenal crisis precipitated by GI losses and missed steroid doses. Cardiac evaluation was reassuring with normal troponin and ECG. He improved significantly after IV stress-dose hydrocortisone and aggressive fluid resuscitation.
goldStandard.diagnosis: Severe dehydration with electrolyte derangements and near-syncope; impending adrenal crisis secondary to missed glucocorticoid doses in the setting of acute gastroenteritis; prerenal acute kidney injury; probable viral or foodborne gastroenteritis
goldStandard.disposition: Admitted to observation unit for continued IV hydration, electrolyte repletion, and stress-dose steroids; transitioned to oral hydrocortisone once tolerating PO; discharged after 18 hours with sick-day steroid dosing instructions, electrolyte recheck in 48 hours, and follow-up with endocrinology and primary care within one week
goldStandard.mdm: This patient's presentation was driven by volume depletion from gastroenteritis compounded by an impending adrenal crisis from missed hydrocortisone doses. The critically low random cortisol of 2.4 confirmed inadequate adrenal reserve. Stress-dose IV hydrocortisone (100 mg bolus then 50 mg q8h) was administered alongside normal saline and potassium/magnesium repletion. Near-syncope was attributed to orthostatic hypotension from dehydration and cortisol deficiency rather than cardiac etiology, supported by negative troponins and a normal ECG. Lactate was mildly elevated but normalized with fluids. He was monitored for hemodynamic stability and adrenal response. Once tolerating oral intake and medications, he was stepped down to double his usual hydrocortisone dose with detailed written sick-day rules. Endocrinology was consulted by phone and agreed with the discharge plan. Family was educated on emergency intramuscular hydrocortisone injection for future GI illness.

title: History of Breast Cancer with New Axillary Mass, Productive Cough, and Fever in a Middle-Aged Woman
doorChart: Female, 40s — cough, fever, and painful axillary lump — ED CDU observation
chiefComplaint: productive cough with fever and new left axillary mass
pmh: History of triple-negative breast cancer with prior lumpectomy, chemotherapy, and radiation; Crohn's disease on biologic therapy; asthma; migraines; history of recurrent urinary tract infections

patientProfile.arrivalMode: self-presented to ED; triaged to CDU for observation
patientProfile.historyOfPresentIllness: I've had this cough going on about ten days now, started dry but now I'm bringing up yellowish stuff. Three days ago I noticed a lump under my left arm — it's sore and getting bigger. Last night I spiked a fever to 101 and I've been having chills and night sweats. My kids had a cold a couple weeks ago and I figured I caught it, but the lump scared me because of my breast cancer. I finished chemo about fourteen months ago and my last scan was clean, but I know things can come back. I'm on Humira for my Crohn's and I know that lowers my immune system. I don't have trouble swallowing or breathing when I'm sitting still, but I get winded going up stairs. No chest pain. I've had some loose stools but that's pretty normal for me with the Crohn's.
patientProfile.socialHistory: Never smoked; does not drink alcohol; divorced; lives with two school-age children; works as a middle school teacher; exercises when energy permits
patientProfile.medications: Adalimumab 40 mg every other week; Mesalamine 1.2 g TID; Montelukast 10 mg daily; Fluticasone/salmeterol 250/50 inhaler BID; Sumatriptan 50 mg PRN; Daily multivitamin
patientProfile.personalityNotes: Articulate and well-informed about her conditions; visibly anxious about cancer recurrence; asks detailed questions; cooperative with all interventions; tearful when discussing possibility of relapse

physicalExam.general: Alert, thin-appearing woman in mild distress; appears flushed and diaphoretic
physicalExam.heent: Oropharynx mildly erythematous with posterior pharyngeal cobblestoning; no exudates; nares with clear rhinorrhea; no cervical lymphadenopathy
physicalExam.cardiovascular: Tachycardic, regular rhythm; no murmurs or gallops; peripheral pulses thready but present
physicalExam.respiratory: Coarse breath sounds left base with dullness to percussion; scattered rhonchi bilaterally; mild intercostal retractions; speaking in full sentences
physicalExam.abdomen: Soft, mild RLQ tenderness (chronic per patient); normoactive bowel sounds; no hepatosplenomegaly
physicalExam.neurological: A&O x3; cranial nerves intact; normal gait
physicalExam.musculoskeletal: Left axillary mass approximately 3x4 cm, firm, tender, with overlying erythema and warmth; no fluctuance; full ROM of left shoulder with discomfort
physicalExam.skin: Flushed; radiation-associated skin changes left breast; no open wounds; no petechiae

labs.name: Metabolic panel
labs.result: Sodium 136 (135-145) | Potassium 3.4 (3.5-5.5) | Chloride 101 | CO2 22 | Glucose 104 (70-100) | BUN 18 | Creatinine 0.7 | Calcium 8.8 (8.5-10.5) | Albumin 3.0 (3.5-5.0) | Alk Phos 112 (<130)

imaging.name: Chest X-ray 2 views
imaging.result: Left lower lobe consolidation with small associated pleural effusion; no pneumothorax; cardiac silhouette normal; mediport in left chest with tip at cavoatrial junction in satisfactory position

imaging.name: Ultrasound left axilla
imaging.result: Heterogeneous solid mass 3.2 x 3.8 cm in left axilla with surrounding soft tissue edema and hypervascularity on Doppler; no drainable fluid collection; morphology indeterminate — could represent reactive lymphadenopathy, infection, or recurrent malignancy; tissue sampling recommended

goldStandard.presentation: A woman in her mid-40s with a history of triple-negative breast cancer in remission and Crohn's disease on immunosuppressive therapy presents with ten days of progressive productive cough, three days of a painful enlarging left axillary mass, and fever with night sweats. Exam reveals left basilar consolidation, tachycardia, borderline hypotension, and a firm tender axillary mass with overlying erythema. Labs show leukocytosis with neutrophilia, elevated procalcitonin and CRP, mild anemia, and hypoalbuminemia. Chest X-ray confirms left lower lobe pneumonia with a small effusion. Axillary ultrasound shows an indeterminate solid mass requiring tissue sampling to differentiate reactive adenopathy, abscess, or cancer recurrence. Given her immunocompromised state, IV antibiotics were started promptly with oncology notified for urgent outpatient biopsy.
goldStandard.diagnosis: Community-acquired pneumonia with left lower lobe consolidation and small parapneumonic effusion; left axillary mass of indeterminate etiology — differential includes reactive lymphadenopathy, soft tissue infection, or recurrent breast malignancy; immunocompromised state secondary to biologic therapy; baseline mild anemia of chronic disease
goldStandard.disposition: Observed overnight in CDU with IV antibiotics (azithromycin and ceftriaxone given penicillin allergy), IV fluids, and bronchodilator therapy; fever defervesced overnight; transitioned to oral antibiotics; discharged with urgent oncology appointment within 5 days for axillary mass biopsy; Humira held pending infectious disease and oncology guidance; return precautions for worsening dyspnea, hemoptysis, or increasing mass size
goldStandard.mdm: This immunocompromised patient presented with community-acquired pneumonia confirmed on imaging and supported by elevated inflammatory markers and procalcitonin. The concomitant axillary mass raised concern for malignancy recurrence versus infectious lymphadenitis in the setting of active pulmonary infection and biologic immunosuppression. Ultrasound was indeterminate, and the team recommended tissue sampling as an outpatient given hemodynamic improvement with fluids and absence of drainable collection. Oncology was contacted and agreed with urgent outpatient biopsy within the week. Adalimumab was held pending resolution of acute infection per infectious disease phone consultation. Blood cultures were pending at discharge but the patient was afebrile and clinically improving. She was transitioned to oral levofloxacin given penicillin allergy and discharged with detailed return precautions. The care team discussed the differential openly with the patient, who expressed understanding and preference for outpatient workup rather than inpatient admission.
